coredata.dc:title: Membranes constructed with zero-dimension carbon quantum dots for CO2 separation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Yue
Zhang, Xinjing
Zhang, Liming
Hu, Linyan
Zhang, Fei
Wang, Yali
Ding, Yunjie
Zhu, Xuefeng
Yang, Weishen
coredata.dc:description: CO2 capture technology provides a straightforward and facile access to address the issue of climate change caused by CO2 emissions from human activities. Membranes separation as a robust strategy can selectively remove CO2 from natural gas and the flue gas of power plants with high economic and practical benefits. Herein, a novel separation membrane based on zero-dimension carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through a simple preparation process for CO2 capture was developed. The CQD membranes with thickness of 100–200 nm upon the substrate show high separation factor in gas separation. The optimal separation factors for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 are up to 81.5 and 94.3 at room temperature, respectively. The permeation process was closely associated with the adsorption and desorption of CO2 as well as the size sieving effect. The CQDs membrane represents an alternative and highly efficient method for CO2 capture.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQD membrane
CO2 capture
Gas separation
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Zero-dimension carbon-based materials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the supported CQD membrane
Preparation and structural analysis of CQD membranes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of N-doped CQDs. (a) Preparation of CQDs through hydrothermal method, (b) image of CQDs powder after freeze drying, (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs in DMF and fluorescence spectrum of CQDs powders, (d) HRTEM image of CQDs, (e) XRD patterns of CQDs powder and CQD membrane after thermal treatment at 350 °C for 10 h and (f) FTIR spectrum of CQDs powder.
(a) XPS survey scan and high-resolution XPS of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s of CQDs powder.
Schematic diagram of CQD membrane and (b) in-situ FTIR spectra and the enlarged E1 and E2 area.
(a) Top and (b) cross-sectional views of SEM images of the supported CQD membrane, (c) EDS mapping of the selected area in (d). (CQDs concentration: 1.8 g L−1, thermal treatment temperature: 350 °C).

(a) Single gas permeance and (b) ideal selectivity determined by the ratios of single gas permeances. (CQDs concentration: 1.8 g L−1, thermal treatment temperature: 350 °C, separation condition: 25 °C and 1 atm.)
(a) CO2/N2 and (b) CO2/CH4 separation factors and permeances depending on the thermal treatment temperature. (CQDs concentration:1.2 g L−1, separation condition: 25 °C and 1 atm.)
(a) CO2/N2 and (b) CO2/CH4 separation factors and permeances depending on CQD concentration. (Thermal treatment temperature: 350 °C, separation condition: 25 °C and 1 atm.)
The binary CO2/N2 performance as a function of CO2 volume fraction in feed gas. (CQDs concentration: 1.8 g L−1, thermal treatment temperature: 350 °C, separation condition: 25 °C and 1 atm.)

(a) Adsorption isotherms of CO2, N2, CH4 and H2 at 15 °C on CQD membrane after thermal treatment; (b) variation of the in situ IR spectra and (c,d) its partial enlarged detail of CQD membrane in the flowing CO2 at 25 °C.

head.ce:title: Membranes constructed with zero-dimension carbon quantum dots for CO2 separation

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Catalysis, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 457 Zhongshan Road, Dalian, 116023, China.
Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Catalysis, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 457 Zhongshan Road, Dalian, 116023, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CO2 capture technology provides a straightforward and facile access to address the issue of climate change caused by CO2 emissions from human activities. Membranes separation as a robust strategy can selectively remove CO2 from natural gas and the flue gas of power plants with high economic and practical benefits. Herein, a novel separation membrane based on zero-dimension carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through a simple preparation process for CO2 capture was developed. The CQD membranes with thickness of 100–200 nm upon the substrate show high separation factor in gas separation. The optimal separation factors for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 are up to 81.5 and 94.3 at room temperature, respectively. The permeation process was closely associated with the adsorption and desorption of CO2 as well as the size sieving effect. The CQDs membrane represents an alternative and highly efficient method for CO2 capture.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dioxide (CO2) in the atmosphere is still increasing rapidly due to the combustion of fossil fuels, becoming the main causes of global warming. To alleviate the greenhouse effect, CO2 capture technologies, containing post-combustion capture [1–3], pre-combustion capture [4–6] and oxy-fuel combustion capture [7,8], have attracted extensive attention in the past few decades. Compared to the traditional cryogenic distillation and absorption separation technologies, membrane-based CO2 capture has many inherent advantages, for instance high efficiency, low energy consumption and readily scale up [5,9,10]. Many materials, such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [10,11], polymers [12], zeolites [13] and carbon-based materials [3,14–17], show high selectivity towards CO2 separation when they are fabricated into membranes. One-, two- and three-dimension carbon-based materials, such as carbon nanotubes [3,14], graphene derivatives [16,17] and carbon molecular sieve (CMS) [15], have been reported as membranes for gas separation. However, membranes based on zero-dimension carbon-based materials are still under developing for gas separation.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are typical zero-dimension spheroids with diameters of 2–10 nm. Abundant functional groups on the surface of CQDs, such as –COOH, –NH2 and –OH, can be used to adjust the surface physical and chemical properties [18–21]. CQDs have been applied in bio-imaging, chemical sensors, medical diagnosis etc. based on their fluorescence (FL) property [19,22–25]. They were also used as additives in membranes for gas separation and wastewater treatment [26–30]. In 2021, Chen et al. prepared mixed matrix membranes with graphene quantum dots as nanofiller for sub-angstrom scale gaseous ethylene/ethane separation, and they found the ethylene/ethane separation factor can be boosted by nearly 60%, reaching up to 99.5 [26]. Zhang et al. prepared nanofiltration membranes for dye rejection by incorporating graphene oxide quantum dots, and achieved high water flux and antifouling [31]. Yu el al. reported a new carbon based separation membranes composed of integrated zero- and one-dimension nanomaterials for carbon capture [32]. However, CQDs in above works either serve as nanofiller among the skeleton layer which was composed of polymers for gas separation, or combined through other small organic molecules for wastewater treatment. As far as we know, CQDs as the skeleton of membrane for gaseous molecular sieving separation has not been reported yet. Theoretically, zero-dimension CQDs could be extended to three-dimension structure via the abundant functional groups on the surface linking with other moieties. The magnitude of size-sieving channels in the three-dimension structure can be regulated by controlling the conditions of cross-linking reactions. Furthermore, the alternative adsorption property of CQDs would be beneficial for the separation of target molecules from complex multicomponent gases based on the modified CQDs with special size, such as CO2, O2, and alkene etc [19,21]. Therefore, it is of great interest to develop CQDs membranes and unravel their intrinsic permeation properties and mechanisms to improve the efficiency of gas separation.

Here, we successfully constructed a new type of separation membranes based on CQDs for CO2 capture. A simple preparation procedure was developed to prepare CQDs membranes by thermal dripping CQDs colloidal solution on porous alumina support and then thermal treating the resultant green membranes at temperature lower than 400 °C under argon. As a novel membrane, the separation factors for binary CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 are up to 81.5 and 94.3, respectively. The permeation process was closely associated with the adsorption of CO2 and size sieving effect.

CQDs were prepared via hydrothermal treatment of citric acid (CA, Sigma-Aldrich, 99%) and ethylenediamine (EDA, Alfa Aesar Chemicals, 99%). Citric acid (9.0 g) and ethylenediamine (3.0 mL) were dissolved in deionized water (30 mL) and stirred for 30 min at room temperature. Then the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 5 h in an oven. After cooling to room temperature, the resultant brown-black mixture was freeze-dried to powder for storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the supported CQD membrane
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S1, CQDs powder was dispersed in N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., AR) to prepare CQD-DMF colloid with different concentrations (1.2, 1.5, 1.8, 2.1, and 2.4 g L−1). Then, 0.1 mL CQD-DMF colloid was dripped on the γ-Al2O3 substrate with 5 nm pore size on the top layer (Inocermic GmbH, Germany) in the oven. The solvent was removed after heating at 100 °C for 5 h. Then, the pretreated membrane was placed into the tubular furnace for further thermal treatment in Ar (100 mL min−1). The temperature was raised to the target temperatures and maintained for 10 h with the heating and cooling rate of 1 °C min−1.

The CQD membranes were installed in a home-made stainless-steel permeation cell and sealed with silicone rubber rings as shown in Fig. S2. The gas permeation and separation performances of membranes were tested using Wicke-Kallenbach method at room temperature. CO2, CH4, N2 and H2 with a flow rate of 100 mL min−1 was used as feed gas in turn to conduct the performance analysis of single component gas after operating the membranes in the binary CO2/N2 for 5 h. CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 mixture with equimolar ratio (total flow rate: 200 mL min−1) was used as feed gas to evaluate the separation performance of CQD membranes for binary gases. Helium (flow rate: 50 mL min−1) was used as the sweep gas. Composition of the effluent gas from permeate side was analyzed on-line by a gas chromatography (Agilent 7890B) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector. The gas permeance was calculated through the following equation,
(1)
P i
=
J i
S
(
p 1
−
p 2
)
where
P i
is the gas permeance (mol m−2 s−1 Pa−1) of component i,
J i
is the flux (mol s−1) of component in the effluent gas. The effective membrane area S is 0.785 cm2.
p 1
and
p 2
are the partial pressure of component i in the feed gas and effluent gas, respectively. The gas permeance is simplified as the unit of GPU (Gas permeation unit) in this paper.
1
GPU = 3.349 × 10−10 mol m−2 s−1 Pa−1

The ideal selectivity for single gas was calculated as follows,
(2)
α
i j
=
P i
P j
where
P i
and
P j
represent the permeance of component i and j respectively.

The separation factor for the mixed gases was calculated as follows,
(3)
α
i j
=
y i
/
y j
x i
/
x j
where
x
and
y
represent the ratio of the concentration of component i and j in the feed and permeate side, respectively.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, PANalytical Empyrean diffractometer, Cu Kα radiation) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermofisher Escalab 250 XI) were used to characterize the structure and composition of CQDs powder and the CQD-DMF powder after thermal treatment. As for CQD-DMF powder, the CQDs powder was dispersed thoroughly in DMF with a concentration of 36 g L−1. The turbid liquid was dried in the oven at 100 °C for 5 h. The CQD-DMF powder was obtained after thermal treatment at 350 °C for 10 h. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted on a Netzsch STA 449F3 thermal analyzer with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 from 50 to 800 °C in N2 with the flow rate of 20 mL min−1.
The CQD-DMF colloid (0.06 g L−1) was used to measure the UV–vis absorption spectrum (UV-1900 UV–vis spectrophotometer). Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed on a PTI QM400 spectrophotometer.

Attenuated total reflection-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) was carried out on Thermo-Fisher Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (HYPERION 3000, Bruker Optics) at a resolution of 4 cm−1 with 32 times scans for observing surface groups of CQDs. The in situ FTIR of supported CQD membrane was conducted on a spectrometer (VERTEX 80V, Bruker Optics). The as-prepared CQD membrane with 0.5 ml CQD-DMF colloid (1.8 g L−1) was dried in the oven at 100 °C for 5 h before the in situ FTIR characterization. The as-prepared CQD membrane was heated in Ar with a flow rate of 10 mL min−1. The temperature was raised to 350 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The nature cooling-off process was set after holding at 350 °C for 5 h. The flowing Ar was replaced by a mixture containing 2% CO2 and 98% Ar in volume fraction after the temperature was cooled to 25 °C. The chamber was heated to 50, 100, 150 and 200 °C in the gas mixture after the determination of CQD membrane at 25 °C. Data collection was carried out every 5 min in the whole process. UV-Raman spectra of CQDs powder and the CQD-DMF powder were collected using a home-built spectrometer. The system was composed of a 325 nm semiconductor laser (Changchun New Industries Optoelectronics Technology Co.), a 25 mm diameter off-axis parabolic mirror (Edmund Optics Co.) as the light-collecting element, an edge filter (Semrock Co.) to filter Rayleigh scattered light, a spectrograph (Shamrock 500), and a UV-CCD camera (Newton 920) produced by Andor. All spectra were calibrated by placing the main Raman peak of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG). The laser power at the sample was kept below 2 mW to prevent burning effects.

The microstructure and morphology of the supported CQD membrane were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7900F). The samples were coated by a 3-nm-thick Pt nanoparticles layer to enhance the conductivity. The roughness of substrate and supported CQD membrane were measured by an atomic force microscope (AFM, NanoWizard). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of CQDs powder and the membrane were collected on a JEM-2100 and F200 thermal-field emission microscope. The sample used for HRTEM observation was prepared via the following procedure. First, a quartz plate was used as support instead of the substrate γ-Al2O3 so that the as-prepared membrane can be peeled off. Then peeled membrane was dispersed in alcohol by ultrasonic wave concussion.
Adsorption of CO2, N2, CH4 and H2 in a range of 0–1 atm at 15 °C was conducted on a Micromeritics (Autosorb iQ).

N-doped CQDs were synthesized from citric acid and ethylenediamine through a hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 a). The brown powders in Fig. 1b were obtained via freeze drying to keep the activity of functional groups on the surface. In Fig. 1c, the UV–vis absorption peaks at 354 nm was assigned to n-π* transition of CO [23,24]. The emission spectra of CQD powders shifts to higher wavelength compared to the excitation spectra, which show the fluorescence characteristics of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1d, the CQDs are ellipsoid nanoparticles with 4–10 nm in diameter and clear lattice fringes. The broad diffraction peak in the XRD pattern of CQDs indicates the formation of graphitized carbon (Fig. 1e) [24]. The functional groups are abundant as shown in the FTIR spectra of CQDs powder in Fig. 1f. The broad strong peaks around 3270 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of N–H or O–H [33]. The sharp peak at 1726 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of CO [33], and 1594 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration of N–H [25]. As shown in Fig. S3, the UV-Raman spectrum of CQDs not shows a typical D and G Raman bands of graphitized carbon [34]. There are several humps on the broad peaks around 1338 cm−1 and 1594 cm−1, indicating the existence of functional groups. C, N and O are the main elements in CQDs, and the atomic percentage of them is 65.1%, 14.2% and 20.7%, respectively (Fig. 2 and Table S1). The high-resolution XPS of C1s, N1s and O1s were fitted and shown in Fig. 2. The fitted peaks at 284.8, 286.3 and 288.2 eV can be respectively assigned to C–C/CC, C–N and CO bonds [35–37]. The same bonds were proved by the fitted peaks located at 400.1 eV and 533.3 eV in the high-resolution XPS of N1s and O1s [36,38]. Besides, N and O are also included in the bulk of CQDs due to the existence of graphite N and NO [39–41]. Thus, we obtained the N-doped CQDs with abundant functional groups on the surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and structural analysis of CQD membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The cross linking reactions among the functional groups (carboxyl, amidogen and hydroxyl) undergo sufficiently, furnishing the three-dimension network structure membrane, namely CQD membrane, as shown in Fig. 3 a. It is a new carbon-based membrane solely formed by zero-dimension CQDs. The detailed procedures of the supported CQD membrane preparation were shown in Fig. S1. Firstly, CQDs powder was dispersed in DMF with proper proportion to form a homogeneous colloid. γ-Al2O3 with 5 nm aperture was chosen as the substrate, considering the occurrence of surficial hydroxyl anchored on γ-Al2O3 and the size of CQDs. Secondly, the semi-finished supported CQD membranes were prepared by dripping CQDs colloidal solution on the substrate in a drying oven at 100 °C. CQDs remain stable in flowing N2 before the heating temperature is lower than 200 °C, as shown in Fig. S4. Thus, after being treated in the drying oven at 100 °C for 5 h in air, higher temperatures for thermal treatment of the semi-finished membranes in the flowing Ar were applied to accelerate the evaporation of solvent and speed up the cross linking reactions between the functional groups. The variation of functional groups of the semi-finished supported CQD membrane during thermal treatment was investigated by in situ FTIR spectra (Fig. 3b). The intensity of part of peaks decreased obviously with the increase of heating temperature due to the reactions between functional groups. The variations in E1 and E2 area are shown in the right enlarged figures. The formation of amide bonds can be inferred from in situ FTIR spectra, because the intensity of peaks at 1700 cm−1, which is assigned to the stretching vibration of CO, and around 3587 cm−1, 1659 cm−1 and 1559 cm−1, which are assigned to the vibration of N–H, amide I and amide II [25,33], respectively, significantly decreased during heating. The appearance of a new peak at 1261 cm−1, which is assigned to the stretching vibration of C–N (Amide III) [33], also supports the existence of amide bonds. Besides the reactions among the functional groups, the carbonized structure is more ordered after thermal treatment, because the CC bending vibration at 966 cm−1 becomes stronger after heating. There are still many functional groups in the membrane after thermal treatment, as shown in the UV-Raman spectra in Fig. S3. It will be beneficial for the adjustment of aperture and adsorption of certain molecules.

The supported CQD membranes are light brown after thermal treatment (Fig. S1), and the color becomes dark with the increase of temperature due to the increase of carbonized degree. The graphitized order structure of CQDs remains after thermal treatment, as shown in the HRTEM image (Fig. S5). The interplanar spacing of (002) is 3.57 Å in the XRD pattern of CQD membrane, which is smaller than that of CQDs (Fig. 1e). The shifting of peak position and narrower peak pattern of COQ membrane imply the enhancement in the carbonized structure after thermal treatment. Compared with CQDs, the relative content of the C element in the CQD membrane increases from 65.1% to 73.1% accompanying with the decrease of the O element content from 20.7% to 11.9% (Table S1 and Fig. S6a). The increase of relative content of CC bond structure and the cleavage of C–N/CN and C–O/CO bond after calcination reveals that the reactions among functional groups and the further carbonization during thermal treatment in the flowing Ar (Fig. 2b–d and Figs. S6b–S6d).

The surface of supported CQD membrane is smooth and defect-free as shown in the micromorphology from the top view in Fig. 4 a. The surface of substrate was covered by a thin membrane without defects. Compared to the bare γ-Al2O3 substrate, the surface roughness of the CQD membrane is decreased according to the AFM images shown in Fig. S7. The membrane thickness was obtained by determining the distribution of elements on the cross section. As shown in the elements distribution map in Fig. 4b and c, the enrichment of N element is unambiguous between the two dotted lines, while the amount of Al in the corresponding area is less than that in the bulk. Thus, the membrane thickness of the sample is around 129 nm. Figs. S8 and S9 show that the membrane thickness increases with the increase in concentration of the CQDs colloid solution.

The transport performance of different gases through the supported CQD membranes was evaluated through the equipment shown in Fig. S2. The performance degradation was found for all the CQD membranes when we started testing. However, the degradation rate gradually decreased with time as shown in Fig. S10. All the permeation tests were carried out after the permeance of CO2 was relatively stable. The permeation tests of single component gases with different dynamic diameters were conducted after operating the membranes in the binary CO2/N2 for 5 h. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the permeance of single component gases decreases with the increase of molecular kinetic diameter, indicating the size sieving effect of the CQD membranes. The sharp cutoff of the permeance between CO2 and N2 reveals that the aperture size of the membrane is between 0.33 and 0.36 nm. The permeance of CO2 is up to 4.3 × 10−8 mol m−2 s−1 Pa−1 (128.4 GPU) at room temperature. The larger molecules, N2 and CH4, are withheld in the feed gas. The average ideal selectivity was calculated based on the single gas permeance of several membranes. As shown in Fig. 5b, the average ideal selectivity of 2.1 for H2/CO2 is lower than the Knudsen diffusion selectivity, indicating the adsorption of CO2 due to the π-bonds in the graphitized CQDs or the basic functional groups on the surface of CQDs. The CQD membranes exhibit an average ideal selectivity of 64.4 for CO2/N2 and 78.5 for CO2/CH4. Such high selectivity of CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 is attributed to both the difference of kinetic diameter and the stronger adsorption of CO2 than N2 and CH4.

The influence of thermal treatment temperature and CQDs concentration on the separation performance were evaluated. The permeation tests of binary gases CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 was conducted after the single gas tests. The permeance of CO2 increases with the thermal treatment temperature due to the formation of more apertures after being treated at a higher temperature as shown in Fig. 6 . The separation factors for the binary gases CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 reach to the maximum at 325 °C and then decrease with the increase of temperature. The optimal separation factors are 55.3 and 50.1 for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4, respectively. The corresponding permeance of CO2 is around 1.0 × 10−8 mol m−2 s−1 Pa−1. Carbon molecular sieve membranes are usually carbonized at high temperatures (>700 °C) and via complicated carbonized process, while the thermal treatment temperature for the novel carbon-based membrane built by zero dimension CQDs is lower than 400 °C. In addition, the preparation process of CQD membrane is far simpler than that of other membranes. The repeatability of CQD membrane is good according to the small error bars of permeation data as shown in Figs. 5–8
. Thus, the CQD membrane is potential to scale up when the repeatability and easy method of membrane preparation are taken into account.

It is expected that the separation factor can be improved by increasing the thickness of the CQD membrane to reduce defects in the membrane. However, to achieve a higher permeance of CO2 for a thick CQD membrane, a higher thermal treatment temperature is necessary. Considering membrane thickness is directly proportional to the concentration of CQDs colloid solution (Fig. 4, S8 and S9), thus 350 °C was selected as the thermal treatment temperature to investigate the influence of concentration of CQDs colloid solution. Fig. 7 shows the variation of separation performance with the increase of CQDs colloid concentration. The selectivities of CQD membranes for both CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 increase with the increase of CQDs concentration when the concentration is lower than 1.8 g L−1. No further increase in separation factor was found when a higher concentration of CQDs colloid was applied as the precursor. It is interesting to find that the concentration of CQDs colloid does not show remarkable influence on the permeance of CO2, although the membrane thickness increased obviously with the concentration as shown in Fig. 4, S8 and S9, implying the bulk diffusion is not the rate determining step of the separation process due to the thin membrane thickness. Considering the dependence of separation factor on the concentration of CQDs colloid, the optimal concentration of CQDs for membrane preparation is 1.8 g L−1, and the optimal separation factors for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 are up to 81.5 (Fig. 8) and 94.3 (Fig. 7b) at room temperature, respectively. The CO2 fraction in the feed gas has little influence on the separation performance of CO2/N2, as shown in Fig. 8. The average separation factor of CO2/N2 is around 75 in a wide range of CO2 fraction and the corresponding permeance of CO2 also not changed, which is important for both CO2-lean and CO2-rich practical applications in CO2 capture.

Adsorption, diffusion and desorption of the gaseous molecular are the main steps for the separation process through a molecular sieving membrane. Molecular diffusion through the CQD membrane has little influence on the separation performance due to the thin membrane thickness, while the adsorption and desorption of CO2 plays an important role in the selective separation of CO2 from the binary CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4. To further disclose the separation mechanism, the adsorptive capacity for single gas was conducted. The adsorptive capacity of CO2 is much higher than N2, CH4 and H2 at 15 °C as shown in Fig. 9 a. The initial permeance of CO2 was even higher than 3.38 × 10−7 mol m−2 s−1 Pa−1 (1007 GPU) because of the high adsorptive capacity of CQD membranes for CO2 (Fig. S10). The in situ FTIR spectra in Fig. 9b–d further proved that CO2 is easy to be adsorbed on the CQD membrane when 2% CO2 (volume ratio in the mixture of CO2/Ar) was injected into the chamber at room temperature. The band intensity of adsorbed CO2 (2354 and 2343 cm−1) strengthened with the time, which implies that the amount of adsorbed CO2 increased [42, 43]. However, there is no obvious change in the structure of CQD membrane except the adsorbed CO2. Thus, it is both adsorption of CO2 and size sieving effect that result in the selective separation of CO2 for CQD membranes. Temperature was gradually increased after the band intensity of adsorbed CO2 reached a stable state. Desorption of CO2 from the CQD membrane did not take place during heating (Fig. S11), which implies the strong adsorption towards CO2, but the whole structure was not destroyed even though the membrane was heated up to 200 °C. The variation is reversible after decreasing to lower temperature and removing CO2 as shown in the enlarged area E1 in Fig. S11. Thus, the structure of CQD membrane is rather stable.

In summary, we prepared N-doped CQDs powders with abundant functional groups through a hydrothermal method. New CQD membranes were successfully developed based on the zero-dimension CQDs as the building blocks via dripping in a drying oven and thermal treatment in flowing inert gas. The membrane shows high separation factor in gas separation and can preferentially permeate CO2 for the binary CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 mixed gases. High separation factors of CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 were obtained after adjusting the thermal treatment temperature and the concentration of CQDs colloid. The selective removal of CO2 is closely related to the high adsorptive capacity of CO2 and the size sieving effect. In addition, it can be expected that various kinds of quantum dots (QDs), such as metal oxide, metal sulfide and CQDs, provide so many possibilities for developing membranes available for gas-gas, liquid-liquid and liquid-solid separation. The potential separation systems of QD membranes can be expanded to many application fields through adjusting the species, size and functional group of QDs.

Yue Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Xinjing Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Liming Zhang: Resources, Investigation, Software. Linyan Hu: Resources, Investigation, Software. Fei Zhang: Resources, Investigation, Software. Yali Wang: Resources, Investigation, Software. Yunjie Ding: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Xuefeng Zhu: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Weishen Yang: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the financial support from YLU-DNL Fund (2022009), National Natural Science Foundation of China (22090063), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M693131) and Social development projects of Shaanxi Science and Technology Department (2021SF-449).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.memsci.2022.121086.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The current status of high temperature electrochemistry-based CO2 transport membranes and reactors for direct CO2 capture and conversion
